blocks.text: I will start by reading the `instructions.md` file to understand the assignment requirements.
I have read the instructions and will now explore the current codebase to understand the existing implementation of the parser, AST, and interpreter.
I will read the `AST.java` and `ValuesTokens.java` files to understand the current AST and token structure.
I'll now examine `ValuesTokens.java`.

I'll now examine `Parser.java` to understand how variables and definitions are currently parsed, keeping in mind the need to update `Variable`, `EmptyConstant`, and remove certain `PrimFun` singletons.
I'll define the `Type` interface in `ValuesTokens.java`, then update `Lexer.java` to recognize the new keywords and types, following an examination of the current `Lexer.java` file.

I'll begin by updating `ValuesTokens.java` to include the `Type` AST and update `EmptyConstant` and `Variable`. I'll also modify `Lexer.java` to allow `:` as a token, add type keywords including `->`, and remove forbidden primitives from the `wordTable`.

input.instruction: Add Type interface and implementations, update Variable and EmptyConstant to include Type, and remove forbidden PrimFuns.

blocks.text: I'll update `ValuesTokens.java` by making `EmptyConstant` non-singleton with a `Type` field, removing forbidden `PrimFun` classes, and updating `PrimFunVisitor`. `Variable` will remain as just a name, while `Def` and `Map` in `AST.java` will be modified to store types at binding sites, as the lexer cannot distinguish between binding and use occurrences.

input.instruction: Update EmptyConstant to include Type and make it non-singleton, and remove forbidden PrimFuns and update PrimFunVisitor.

blocks.text: I'll update `AST.java` to support types by introducing a `TypedId` class, consisting of a `Variable` and a `Type`. I'll then modify `Map` to use `TypedId[]` and `Def` to use `TypedId` for their respective variables.

input.instruction: Add TypedId class and update Map and Def to use it. Also update Let.vars() and Let.exps().

blocks.text: I'll now update `Lexer.java` by adding `COLON`, `ARROW`, and new type keywords as tokens, either as `KeyWord` instances or new `Token` classes.

input.instruction: Add COLON and ARROW tokens, and type keywords. Remove forbidden primitives from wordTable. Update readToken to handle ':' and '->'.

blocks.text: I'll update `Lexer.readToken()` to correctly handle `->` and `:`, and I'll also modify `initWordTable` to remove forbidden primitives and include the new keywords.

input.instruction: Update readToken to handle '->' and ':' and update initWordTable to include new type keywords and remove forbidden primitives.

blocks.text: I'll now update `Parser.java` to accommodate the new syntax and types: `empty` becomes `empty : Type`, `Prim` must be followed by `( ExpList )`, `parseMap` will use `TypedIdList`, `parseDef` will use `TypedId := Exp ;`, and I'll implement a new `parseType` method.

I'll update `Parser.java` by implementing `parseTerm` to handle `EmptyConstant` and `PrimFun`, defining a `parseType` method to process types like `unit`, `int`, `bool`, `list`, `ref`, and function types, and adding `parseTypedId`, `parseVars`, and `parseDef` to support the new typed syntax. I'll also ensure that `Lexer.REF` is correctly handled in `parseType` using identity comparison.

I'll implement the `checkTypes` method in the `Interpreter` class by creating a `TypeChecker` class that implements `ASTVisitor<Type>`, after first defining `TypeException`. I also need to simplify `Interp.java` to support only `eagerEval()` and `lazyEval()`, as specified in Step 1, following an examination of the current `Interp.java` file.

I need to overhaul `Interp.java` by simplifying the `Interpreter` API to only include `eagerEval()` and `lazyEval()`, which must now call a new `checkTypes(AST)` method before evaluation. I also need to update `EvalVisitor` to accommodate changes in `AST`, such as `TypedId[]` returns, and remove forbidden primitives. Finally, I'll define `TypeException` and update `Syntax.java` to handle the modified `Let.vars()` and `Map.vars()`.

I'll update `Syntax.java` to accommodate `Map.vars()` and `Let.vars()` returning `TypedId[]`. I'll achieve this by extracting `Variable` arrays from the `TypedId` arrays within the `forMap` and `forLet` methods.

I'll implement `checkTypes` and the `TypeChecker` visitor in `Interp.java`, following the instructions to invoke the parser, syntax checker, type checker, and then the evaluation method in `eagerEval()` and `lazyEval()`. The `TypeChecker` will use a type environment to verify constants, variables, applications (including polymorphic primitives), and recursive `let` bindings, ensuring all types match as required. I'll also update `EvalVisitor` to handle the new `TypedId[]` return from `map.vars()` and rewrite `Interp.java` to fulfill all assignment requirements.
I'll verify that I haven't missed any `PrimFun` in the `AST.java` or `ValuesTokens.java` visitors, and then I'll run the tests to ensure `Interp.java` compiles correctly.

blocks.text: `1(2)` triggered a `ParseException` because `Int` isn't a `Factor` according to the grammar. To properly test for a `TypeException`, I'll use `let x:int := 1; in x(2)`, as `x` is an `Id` and thus a `Factor`, but its type won't allow application.

input.instruction: Change 1(2) to let x:int := 1; in x(2) to trigger TypeException instead of ParseException.

blocks.text: `1(2)` is indeed a syntax error since `Int` isn't a `Factor`. Rerunning tests.

All tests passed, and I've verified the implementation of `empty : Type`, the removal of forbidden primitives, and the enforcement of `Prim` argument lists. I've also confirmed that `Unop` and `Binop` parsing and type checking, including polymorphic equality and recursive `let` semantics, are correct. My `Interpreter` now correctly provides only `eagerEval()` and `lazyEval()`, having overwritten the previous version. I'll now double-check the `Interp.java` content to ensure everything is as intended.

I've confirmed my implementation follows all instructions: the evaluation order is Parser -> Syntax Check -> Type Check -> Eval; polymorphic types are correctly handled; and `Prim` operations cannot be used as data values. I've also verified that `ref` is correctly parsed as a `UnOp` and can't be used as a standalone value. I'll keep my agent tests in `tests/agent/` as requested and perform a final test run before submitting.
Ready to submit.
